claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess this, I'll need to gather some information about your circumstances and any children you're responsible for.

Let me start with a few questions:

**What is the name and age of the child or children you're inquiring about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Thanks for helping me with this. I'm asking about my child Alex, who is 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you're asking about Alex, who is 8 years old. That's helpful.

Now, to determine your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to understand your living situation:

**Do you currently live with Alex, or does Alex live with someone else?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you for confirming that. Since Alex lives with you, that's one of the key eligibility requirements.

Now I need to ask about your residency:

**Do you live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent. That covers the main residency and living arrangement requirements.

Now I need to check one more important thing about your circumstances:

**Do you have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or are you a UK citizen/have indefinite leave to remain in the UK?**

(This helps me understand if there are any immigration status factors that might affect your eligibility.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I'm a UK citizen, so that's all sorted.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Thank you for clarifying that. You've confirmed all the key eligibility requirements so far.

Just to make sure I have a complete picture, let me ask one final question:

**Is Alex currently in any of the following situations: in hospital for more than 12 weeks, in care for more than 8 weeks, or in residential accommodation?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has been in hospital for a while now - about 3 to 4 months actually. But I still regularly spend money on things for them, you know, like their favorite snacks, books, little things to keep them comfortable while they're in there.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that important information. I have all the details I need to make a determination about your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me explain my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Good news! You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:

You meet all the standard eligibility requirements:
- Alex is 8 years old (under 16)
- You live in the UK
- You live with Alex
- You are a UK citizen

The important factor in your case is that although Alex has been in hospital for 3-4 months (which exceeds the normal 12-week limit), you qualify for an **exception** to the usual rules. Because you are regularly spending money on Alex's upkeep while in hospital—such as snacks, books, and comfort items—you can continue to receive Child Benefit.

If you haven't already done so, I'd recommend notifying HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) about Alex's hospital stay so they have this information on file and can ensure your payments continue appropriately.

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old and under 16, so meets the age requirement. You are a UK citizen living in the UK and live with Alex, meeting the residency and responsibility requirements. Although Alex has been in hospital for 3-4 months (exceeding the standard 12-week limit after which Child Benefit normally stops), there is an exception to this rule. According to the guidance, for children in a UK hospital, if you regularly spend money on them, you can continue to receive Child Benefit. You have confirmed that you regularly spend money on Alex's upkeep while in hospital (snacks, books, comfort items). Therefore, you qualify for the exception and remain eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. You meet all the core requirements: you are a UK citizen, you live in the UK, you are responsible for Alex (living with them), and Alex is under 16. Although Alex's hospital stay has exceeded the standard 12-week threshold, you qualify for the exception because you are regularly spending money on Alex's upkeep while in hospital. This ongoing financial contribution means you can continue to receive Child Benefit."

You are a UK citizen living in the UK and live with Alex, meeting the residency and responsibility requirements. Although Alex has been in hospital for 3-4 months (exceeding the standard 12-week limit after which Child Benefit normally stops), there is an exception to this rule. According to the guidance, for children in a UK hospital, if you regularly spend money on them, you can continue to receive Child Benefit. You have confirmed that you regularly spend money on Alex's upkeep while in hospital (snacks, books, comfort items). Therefore, you qualify for the exception and remain eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex. You meet all the core requirements: you are a UK citizen, you live in the UK, you are responsible for Alex (living with them), and Alex is under 16. Although Alex's hospital stay has exceeded the standard 12-week threshold, you qualify for the exception because you are regularly spending money on Alex's upkeep while in hospital. This ongoing financial contribution means you can continue to receive Child Benefit."
